track.label: Synthesis Track — gpt-5.6-sol
track.hypothesis: Combine the strongest independently judged universal mechanism with accepted cross-track localization lessons, then run a second tightening pass.
track.decision_policy: Synthesis uses provisional self-scores only and remains keep_for_synthesis pending independent final-panel review.

scorers.notes: Provisional self-scores only; not an external panel.

iterations.hypothesis: A fresh walls/corner Q&A that keeps the panel-majority geometry while repairing Spanish inner-corner vocabulary and Japanese counting/punctuation seams will outperform an unchanged parent without sacrificing the visual mechanism favored in the panel dissent.
iterations.outcome: Produced a fresh localized wall/corner candidate and passed completeness. Spanish and Japanese panel defects were directly repaired, but explicit adjacency wording made English and French less brief than necessary, creating a concrete Loop 2 target.

commands.build: Synthesized a new four-language wall/corner candidate from all parent lessons and blind-panel feedback.
commands.run: Inspected every setup, punchline, combined form, native script, and panel-noted lexical seam.
commands.judge: Applied the five-criterion rubric as provisional synthesis self-scoring only; no new external panel scores were created.

scores.notes: Same adjacent-walls/physical-corner/rendezvous mechanism in all four languages.
Classic clean Q&A personification with a literal geometric reveal.
Compact overall, but explicit adjacency makes English and French longer than needed.
Requires only common walls and corners.
Spanish and Japanese seams are fixed; English/French remain slightly explanatory.

completeness_gate.notes: All four language objects contain setup, punchline, combined, and portability_notes; Japanese contains kanji and kana.

completeness.notes: All four language objects contain setup, punchline, combined, and portability_notes; Japanese contains kanji and kana.

prompt.track_prompt:
<current_datetime>2026-07-20T21:34:00.131+10:00</current_datetime>

You are the synthesis Track in a formal Experiment Loop. Your actual model ID is gpt-5.6-sol. Work autonomously. You may READ all files under C:\repos\chrisauld_microsoft\ai-strategy\copilot-worktrees\experiment-loop-skill\chrisauld-microsoft-bookish-system\.regeneration-6ydsfscu\multilingual-dad-joke\workspace\generated\track-gpt-5-5\, ...\generated\track-gemini-3-1-pro-preview\, ...\generated\track-claude-sonnet-5\, and ...\generated\panel\. WRITE only under C:\repos\chrisauld_microsoft\ai-strategy\copilot-worktrees\experiment-loop-skill\chrisauld-microsoft-bookish-system\.regeneration-6ydsfscu\multilingual-dad-joke\workspace\generated\synthesis\. Do not modify source tracks, panel outputs, build_viewer.py, prompt.md, or .github.

Goal: best wholesome dad joke natural in English, French, Spanish, Japanese; universal mechanism preferred over English-only wordplay. Scorecard 1-5 equal weights: cross_language_equivalence, dad_joke_groan, brevity, cultural_portability, translation_naturalness. Blocking completeness: all four languages in native script, Japanese in Japanese script.

The blind panel evaluated six anonymized source candidates. Candidate mapping (now unblinded for synthesis): A=gpt-5.5 loop-02 shadow; B=claude loop-01 hungry; C=gemini loop-02 walls/corner; D=gpt-5.5 loop-01 echo; E=claude loop-02 tired/name; F=gemini loop-01 high-eyebrows/surprised. Panel rankings: claude-opus-4.8 C>A>D>F>E>B; gpt-5.6-terra C>F>D>A>B>E; gemini-3.1-pro-preview F>C>A>D>E>B. Preserve this dissent: two judges prefer C for tighter classic dad-joke structure; Gemini prefers F as a flawless visual gag. All reject B/E as calque traps.

Run exactly TWO synthesis loops. Loop 1 MUST have parent_ids ["track-gpt-5-5-loop-02","track-gemini-3-1-pro-preview-loop-02","track-claude-sonnet-5-loop-02"] and explicitly inspect all three parent Tracks' both loops, self-critiques/lessons, and the three panel score files. Create acceptance-matrix.json with one entry per parent Track containing lessons_accepted (with evidence and how applied) and lessons_rejected (with evidence and why), so no parent is omitted. Build a fresh multilingual candidate, not a copy without refinement. Save loop-01/candidate.json, draft.md, self-critique.md, loop.json. The loop record must include actual model_id gpt-5.6-sol, exact full prompt.track_prompt, input_feedback quoting/including the parent and panel feedback actually used, judge_feedback containing the panel feedback used, and next_prompt. Include hypothesis, outcome, provisional scores, completeness gate, one durable lesson, decision keep_for_synthesis.

Loop 2 MUST consume loop 1 self-critique and materially improve the same candidate/mechanism, save analogous files with id synthesis-loop-02, track_id synthesis, parent_ids ["synthesis-loop-01"], model_id gpt-5.6-sol. Candidate JSON: id, mechanism, languages.en/fr/es/ja each setup/punchline/combined/portability_notes, revision_from/revision_reason. Keep translations idiomatic; specifically address panel-noted seams for walls (Spanish esquina vs inner rincón; Japanese stilted wall counting/quotes) if selecting that mechanism, or panel-noted length/pun looseness if selecting eyebrows. Do not invent external panel scores; use provisional self-scores only pending final panel.

Finally write synthesis-report.md explaining each accepted/rejected parent lesson, blind-panel dissent, both loops, remaining limitations, and next highest-value experiment; manifest-fragment.json with track definition and both loop records; status.json. Ensure all JSON valid UTF-8. Do not stop after one attempt.
prompt.input_feedback:
Parent Track evidence actually used after inspecting both loops, drafts, candidates, self-critiques, and loop lessons:
- track-gpt-5-5 loop 01 lesson: “Prefer a concrete, visually obvious universal property that creates the failure directly, not a property plus an added social role.” Loop 02 lesson: “For multilingual dad jokes, a physical object failing at an ordinary task can be funnier and more portable than translated wordplay or idiomatic double meanings.” Its shadow candidate remained limited by a “simple” punchline and panel-described mild groan.
- track-gemini-3-1-pro-preview loop 01 lesson: “Visual narrative jokes translate perfectly but may lack the interactive rhythm of a classic dad joke. Q&A format is culturally expected for the highest ‘dad joke groan’ score.” Loop 02 lesson: “Personification of inanimate objects based on universal physical or geometric properties ... is the most effective mechanism for creating cross-lingual dad jokes.” The loop-02 self-critique claimed perfect naturalness, but the panel identified Spanish and Japanese seams.
- track-claude-sonnet-5 loop 01 lesson: choose an anchor only after checking whether every language shares the grammatical shape. Loop 02 lesson: “When a comic mechanism depends on a specific grammatical pattern ... audit every target language’s idiomatic phrasing.” The panel nevertheless found “Salut Fatigué,” “Hola Cansado,” and “はじめまして疲れた” calque-like or unnatural, so the general audit lesson is useful while the name mechanism is not.

Blind-panel feedback actually used:
- judge-claude-opus-4.8 ranked C>A>D>F>E>B. For C: “Quintessential call-and-response dad-joke format with a universal physical pun” and “Spanish ‘esquina’ denotes an outer corner where walls form an inner ‘rincón’; a minor semantic seam.” For F: “Longest setup of the set; two-clause narrative reduces brevity.” It rejected B/E because the name parse becomes unnatural, especially in Japanese.
- judge-gpt-5.6-terra ranked C>F>D>A>B>E. For C: “The physical fact that two walls meet at a corner supplies the same compact, familiar groaner in every language,” while noting that the Japanese “one wall ... another wall” construction is stilted and that quotation marks should be removed. For F it noted marginally greater length and looser French/Spanish pun-like wording.
- judge-gemini-3.1-pro-preview ranked F>C>A>D>E>B. It called F “A perfect visual dad joke” with “None” listed as defects; it still called C a “Strong contender” with a double meaning that translates well. Its panel note favored visual/situational and physical mechanisms over grammatical wordplay.
Preserved dissent: two judges preferred C for its tighter classic Q&A dad-joke structure; Gemini preferred F as a flawless visual gag. All three rejected B/E as calque traps.

- judge-gpt-5.6-terra ranked C>F>D>A>B>E. For C: “The physical fact that two walls meet at a corner supplies the same compact, familiar groaner in every language,” while noting that the Japanese “one wall ... another wall” construction is stilted and that quotation marks should be removed. For F it noted marginally greater length and looser French/Spanish pun-like wording.
- judge-gemini-3.1-pro-preview ranked F>C>A>D>E>B. It called F “A perfect visual dad joke” with “None” listed as defects; it still called C a “Strong contender” with a double meaning that translates well. Its panel note favored visual/situational and physical mechanisms over grammatical wordplay.
Preserved dissent: two judges preferred C for its tighter classic Q&A dad-joke structure; Gemini preferred F as a flawless visual gag. All three rejected B/E as calque traps.
prompt.next_prompt: Keep the walls-meet-at-the-corner mechanism. Remove explanatory adjacency wording where each language has a shorter native riddle formula, align the setup as a timeless present-tense question where natural, retain Spanish `rincón`, and keep Japanese free of numeric wall counting and quotation marks. Re-check all five criteria and the completeness gate; use provisional self-scores only.

lesson.trigger: Repairs for Spanish and Japanese exposed new explanatory padding in English and French.
lesson.action: Localize each setup to its shortest native riddle formula while preserving the shared physical inference.
lesson.evidence: Loop 01 provisionally scores 5 for equivalence and groan but only 4 for brevity and naturalness because of “the wall next to it” and `mur d’à côté`.

iterations.hypothesis: Shortening each setup to its native generic-riddle form while retaining the Spanish and Japanese seam repairs will materially improve brevity and naturalness without changing the walls/corner mechanism.
iterations.outcome: The same mechanism was materially tightened in all four languages. English, French, and Spanish lost explanatory adjacency padding; Japanese moved to a concise generic present while retaining the no-counter/no-quotes repair. Completeness passes, with only provisional self-scores pending independent review.

commands.build: Revised the Loop 01 wall/corner candidate using the full Loop 01 self-critique.
commands.run: Compared Loop 01 and Loop 02 field by field and checked native scripts, required fields, punctuation, and known panel seams.
commands.judge: Applied the five-criterion rubric as provisional synthesis self-scoring only; preserved prior panel dissent without inventing a new panel result.

scores.notes: All versions preserve the wall/rendezvous/physical-corner inference.
Explanatory adjacency padding was removed in every language where a shorter native form exists.
Common walls, corners, and making plans require no specific cultural knowledge.
Localized riddle forms retain Spanish rincón and natural Japanese without counters or quotes.

completeness_gate.notes: All four language objects contain setup, punchline, combined, and portability_notes; Japanese contains kanji and kana.

completeness.notes: All four language objects contain setup, punchline, combined, and portability_notes; Japanese contains kanji and kana.

prompt.input_feedback:
Loop 2 consumed the complete Loop 1 self-critique:

# Self-Critique: synthesis loop 01

## Evidence consumed
All six parent loops and self-critiques were inspected, along with the three blind panel score files. The acceptance and rejection decisions are itemized in `../acceptance-matrix.json`.

## What changed
- Kept the majority-preferred walls/corner geometry and classic Q&A structure.
- Replaced Spanish `esquina` with `rincón`, the inner corner formed by walls.
- Replaced Japanese `一つの壁がもう一つの壁に…` with colloquial `壁が隣の壁に…`.
- Removed Japanese quotation marks.
- Made adjacency explicit in all setups rather than copying the source wording.

## Provisional synthesis self-scores
- cross_language_equivalence: 5/5 — Every version personifies adjacent walls and resolves at their physical meeting point.
- dad_joke_groan: 5/5 — The compact literalized rendezvous is a classic clean Q&A groaner.
- brevity: 4/5 — Still compact, but “the wall next to it” / `mur d’à côté` adds avoidable setup length.
- cultural_portability: 5/5 — Walls, adjacency, and corners require no local knowledge.
- translation_naturalness: 4/5 — Spanish and Japanese seams are repaired, but English/French are slightly explanatory, and the four setups mix generic and conversational forms.

These are provisional self-scores only; no external score is claimed for the synthesis candidate.

## Completeness gate
PASS. English, French, Spanish, and Japanese are present. Japanese contains kanji and kana (`壁`, `隣`, `角`, `で`, `会おう`). French uses required apostrophes/accents and spacing; Spanish uses inverted question/exclamation punctuation and `rincón`.

## Remaining defect
The explicit adjacency repair over-corrects the source: it makes English and French wordier than necessary. The languages also use slightly different tense framing. Natural localization should preserve the mechanism, not force identical syntax.

## Durable lesson
When panel feedback exposes a lexical seam, repair the local term without padding every language with explanatory wording; semantic equivalence matters more than literal structural symmetry.

## Next prompt
Keep the walls-meet-at-the-corner mechanism. Remove explanatory adjacency wording where each language has a shorter native riddle formula, align the setup as a timeless present-tense question where natural, retain Spanish `rincón`, and keep Japanese free of numeric wall counting and quotation marks. Re-check all five criteria and the completeness gate; use provisional self-scores only.

The prior blind-panel feedback remained a constraint, not a new score:

Blind-panel feedback actually used:
- judge-claude-opus-4.8 ranked C>A>D>F>E>B. For C: “Quintessential call-and-response dad-joke format with a universal physical pun” and “Spanish ‘esquina’ denotes an outer corner where walls form an inner ‘rincón’; a minor semantic seam.” For F: “Longest setup of the set; two-clause narrative reduces brevity.” It rejected B/E because the name parse becomes unnatural, especially in Japanese.
- judge-gpt-5.6-terra ranked C>F>D>A>B>E. For C: “The physical fact that two walls meet at a corner supplies the same compact, familiar groaner in every language,” while noting that the Japanese “one wall ... another wall” construction is stilted and that quotation marks should be removed. For F it noted marginally greater length and looser French/Spanish pun-like wording.
- judge-gemini-3.1-pro-preview ranked F>C>A>D>E>B. It called F “A perfect visual dad joke” with “None” listed as defects; it still called C a “Strong contender” with a double meaning that translates well. Its panel note favored visual/situational and physical mechanisms over grammatical wordplay.
Preserved dissent: two judges preferred C for its tighter classic Q&A dad-joke structure; Gemini preferred F as a flawless visual gag. All three rejected B/E as calque traps.
prompt.judge_feedback:
No new external judging was performed. The panel feedback carried forward and used in this revision was:

Blind-panel feedback actually used:
- judge-claude-opus-4.8 ranked C>A>D>F>E>B. For C: “Quintessential call-and-response dad-joke format with a universal physical pun” and “Spanish ‘esquina’ denotes an outer corner where walls form an inner ‘rincón’; a minor semantic seam.” For F: “Longest setup of the set; two-clause narrative reduces brevity.” It rejected B/E because the name parse becomes unnatural, especially in Japanese.
- judge-gpt-5.6-terra ranked C>F>D>A>B>E. For C: “The physical fact that two walls meet at a corner supplies the same compact, familiar groaner in every language,” while noting that the Japanese “one wall ... another wall” construction is stilted and that quotation marks should be removed. For F it noted marginally greater length and looser French/Spanish pun-like wording.
- judge-gemini-3.1-pro-preview ranked F>C>A>D>E>B. It called F “A perfect visual dad joke” with “None” listed as defects; it still called C a “Strong contender” with a double meaning that translates well. Its panel note favored visual/situational and physical mechanisms over grammatical wordplay.
Preserved dissent: two judges preferred C for its tighter classic Q&A dad-joke structure; Gemini preferred F as a flawless visual gag. All three rejected B/E as calque traps.

Loop 1 provisional self-judge feedback used: shorten “the wall next to it” / `mur d’à côté`, preserve Spanish `rincón`, preserve Japanese `隣の壁`, remove numeric counting and quotation marks, and align native riddle framing.
prompt.next_prompt: Submit synthesis-loop-02 to a fresh blind panel in randomized order against the original wall candidate C and visual eyebrow candidate F. Ask judges to focus on whether Spanish `rincón` preserves the rendezvous beat and whether the concise Japanese line sounds spontaneous; do not promote provisional self-scores as external results.

lesson.trigger: Loop 01’s literal symmetry preserved the mechanism but reduced brevity in English and French.
lesson.action: Preserve comic inference while choosing the shortest native setup and the physically correct local corner term.
lesson.evidence: Loop 02 removes adjacency padding, keeps Spanish `rincón`, and keeps Japanese counter/quote repairs; provisional brevity and naturalness rise from 4 to 5.

iterations.stop_reason: Exactly two synthesis loops requested; stop after Loop 02 pending independent final-panel review.
